title: Needles, California

infobox.value: is a city in [San Bernardino County](/wiki/San_Bernardino_County "San Bernardino County"), in the [Mojave Desert](/wiki/Mojave_Desert "Mojave Desert") region of [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California"). Situated on the western banks of the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River"), Needles is located near the California border with [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") and [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada").[[5]](#cite_note-NeedlesDevPlanDoc-5) The city is accessible via [Interstate 40](/wiki/Interstate_40_in_California "Interstate 40 in California") and [U.S. Route 95](/wiki/U.S._Route_95_in_California "U.S. Route 95 in California"). The population was 4,931 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), up from 4,844 at the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_Census "2010 United States Census").

**Needles** is a city in [San Bernardino County](/wiki/San_Bernardino_County "San Bernardino County"), in the [Mojave Desert](/wiki/Mojave_Desert "Mojave Desert") region of [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California"). Situated on the western banks of the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River"), Needles is located near the California border with [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") and [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada").[[5]](#cite_note-NeedlesDevPlanDoc-5) The city is accessible via [Interstate 40](/wiki/Interstate_40_in_California "Interstate 40 in California") and [U.S. Route 95](/wiki/U.S._Route_95_in_California "U.S. Route 95 in California"). The population was 4,931 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), up from 4,844 at the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_Census "2010 United States Census").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Frashers_photo_%28NBY_433339%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Frashers_photo_%28NBY_433339%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frashers_photo_(NBY_433339)_(cropped).jpg)

Downtown Needles, c. 1930s

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/California_-_Needles_-_NARA_-_23934695.jpg/250px-California_-_Needles_-_NARA_-_23934695.jpg)](/wiki/File:California_-_Needles_-_NARA_-_23934695.jpg)

Aerial view of Needles, 1930s

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/California_-_Needles_-_NARA_-_23934693.jpg/250px-California_-_Needles_-_NARA_-_23934693.jpg)](/wiki/File:California_-_Needles_-_NARA_-_23934693.jpg)

[AT&SF](/wiki/AT%26SF "AT&SF") rail yards in Needles, 1942

The [Mojave people](/wiki/Mojave_people "Mojave people") first inhabited the area.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Needles was founded in May 1883 during the construction of the [Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railway](/wiki/Atchison,_Topeka_and_Santa_Fe_Railway "Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railway"),[[7]](#cite_note-VDP_2020-01-19-7) which originally crossed the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") at [Eastbridge, Arizona](/wiki/Eastbridge,_Arizona "Eastbridge, Arizona"), three miles southeast of modern Needles. Needles was named after ["The Needles"](/wiki/The_Needles_(Arizona) "The Needles (Arizona)"), a group of [pinnacles](/wiki/Pinnacle_(geology) "Pinnacle (geology)") in the [Mohave Mountains](/wiki/Mohave_Mountains "Mohave Mountains") on the Arizona side of the river. The crossing was a poor site for a bridge, lacking firm banks and a solid bottom.[[8]](#cite_note-Lingenfelter-8): 82

A bridge was built, but it was of poor quality. Not only was it a "flimsy looking structure", but it was an obstacle to navigation on the river. Flooding on the Colorado River destroyed the bridge three times – in 1884, 1886 and 1888. The railway built [Red Rock Bridge](/wiki/Red_Rock_Bridge "Red Rock Bridge"), a high [cantilever bridge](/wiki/Cantilever_bridge "Cantilever bridge"), at a narrower point with solid rock footings, ten miles downstream near today's [Topock](/wiki/Topock,_Arizona "Topock, Arizona"). The bridge was completed in May 1890, and the old bridge was dismantled.[[8]](#cite_note-Lingenfelter-8): 82

At first it was a tent town for railroad construction crews, but the railway would eventually build a hotel, car sheds, shops and a roundhouse. Within only a month, Needles would have a Chinese laundry, a newsstand, a restaurant, several general stores, and nine or ten saloons. Needles quickly became the largest port on the river above [Yuma, Arizona](/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona").[[8]](#cite_note-Lingenfelter-8): 82 The railway and the [Fred Harvey Company](/wiki/Fred_Harvey_Company "Fred Harvey Company") built the elegant [Neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") and [Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture") style [El Garces Hotel](/wiki/El_Garces_Hotel "El Garces Hotel") and Santa Fe Station in 1908, which was considered the "crown jewel" of the entire Fred Harvey chain.[[7]](#cite_note-VDP_2020-01-19-7) The landmark building is on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") and is being restored.

Needles was a major stop on the historic [U.S. Route 66](/wiki/U.S._Route_66 "U.S. Route 66") highway from the 1920s through the 1960s.[[9]](#cite_note-9) For migrants from the Midwest [Dust Bowl](/wiki/Dust_Bowl "Dust Bowl") in the 1930s, it was the town that marked their arrival in California. The city is lined with [motels](/wiki/Motel "Motel") and other shops from that era. The "Carty's Camp", which appears briefly in *[The Grapes of Wrath](/wiki/The_Grapes_of_Wrath_(film) "The Grapes of Wrath (film)")* as the Joad family enters California from [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"),[[7]](#cite_note-VDP_2020-01-19-7) is now a ghost tourist court, its remains located behind the 1940s-era [66 Motel](/wiki/66_Motel_(Needles) "66 Motel (Needles)").

In 1949, the [United States Bureau of Reclamation](/wiki/United_States_Bureau_of_Reclamation "United States Bureau of Reclamation") began an extensive project to dredge a new channel for the Colorado River that would straighten out a river bend that caused serious silt problems after the Hoover Dam was completed.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Needles is a tourism and recreation center.[[7]](#cite_note-VDP_2020-01-19-7) The city is the eastern gateway to the [Mojave National Preserve](/wiki/Mojave_National_Preserve "Mojave National Preserve"), a scenic desert area.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 31.1 square miles (81 km2). 30.6 square miles (79 km2) of it is land and 0.5 square miles (1.3 km2) of it (1.60%) is water.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Colorado_River_at_Needles_1.jpg/250px-Colorado_River_at_Needles_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colorado_River_at_Needles_1.jpg)

The [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") separates Needles, in California, from [Mohave Valley](/wiki/Mohave_Valley,_Arizona "Mohave Valley, Arizona"), in [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona").

The city has a [desert climate](/wiki/Desert_climate#Hot_desert_climate "Desert climate") with a [subtropical](/wiki/Subtropical_climate "Subtropical climate") temperature range, with a mean annual temperature of 76.2 °F (24.6 °C).[[11]](#cite_note-wrcc-11)

Needles, like [Death Valley](/wiki/Death_Valley "Death Valley") to the northwest, is known for extreme heat during the summer. The Needles weather station is frequently reported by the United States government's [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration](/wiki/National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration "National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration") (NOAA) as the site of the highest daily temperature recorded in the U.S. during the desert summers. Needles occasionally sets national or world daily temperature records, along with other related records associated with extreme desert heat. For instance, on July 22, 2006, Needles experienced a record high low temperature of 100 °F (37.8 °C) at 6:00 am with a high temperature exceeding 120 °F (48.9 °C),[[12]](#cite_note-Weather_Underground-12) making it one of the few locations on Earth that have recorded an overnight minimum temperature higher than 100 °F (37.8 °C).

On August 13, 2012, Needles experienced a thunderstorm that deposited rain at a temperature of 115 °F (46.1 °C) starting at 3:56 pm, setting a new record for the hottest rain in world history. The air temperature was 118 °F (47.8 °C), tying Needles's record high for the date. Since the humidity was only 11%, the rain evaporated so that "only a trace of precipitation was recorded in the rain gauge". Weather records researcher Maximiliano Herrera reported that this was the lowest humidity at which rain has occurred on Earth in recorded history.[[13]](#cite_note-13) On May 4, 2014, Needles reached a temperature of 102 °F (38.9 °C) with a [dewpoint](/wiki/Dewpoint "Dewpoint") of −38 °F (−38.9 °C), for a [relative humidity](/wiki/Relative_humidity "Relative humidity") of 0.33%, the lowest value ever recorded on Earth.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In the winter, temperatures are typically mild, with December, the coolest month, having a normal mean temperature of 54.7 °F (12.6 °C). The hottest month, July, has a normal mean temperature of 98.5 °F (36.9 °C).[[15]](#cite_note-NOWData_NWS_Las_Vegas,_NV_(VEF)_-_EEDthr-15) On average, there are 119 days annually with a maximum of 100 °F (37.8 °C) or higher, 175 days with a maximum of 90 °F (32.2 °C) or higher, and 2.7 days with a minimum of 32 °F (0 °C) or lower.[[16]](#cite_note-NCDC_pdf-16) Official record temperatures range from 18 °F (−7.8 °C) on January 22, 1937, to 125 °F (51.7 °C), last recorded on June 20, 2017.[[15]](#cite_note-NOWData_NWS_Las_Vegas,_NV_(VEF)_-_EEDthr-15)[[17]](#cite_note-wundergroundwave-17)

Annual normal rainfall is 4.32 inches (109.7 mm), and there is an annual normal of 23 days with measurable precipitation.[[16]](#cite_note-NCDC_pdf-16) The wettest year was 1939 with 13.33 inches (338.6 mm) of rainfall and the driest year was 2006 with 0.7 inches (17.8 mm). The most rainfall in one month was 7.61 inches (193.3 mm) in September 1939.[[15]](#cite_note-NOWData_NWS_Las_Vegas,_NV_(VEF)_-_EEDthr-15) The most rainfall in a calendar day was 3.49 inches (88.6 mm) on August 19, 1906.[[15]](#cite_note-NOWData_NWS_Las_Vegas,_NV_(VEF)_-_EEDthr-15) Snowfall is very rare in Needles, with the only month recording measurable snowfall being January 1949, when 15.2 inches (38.6 cm) of snow fell, including 12.2 inches (31 cm) inches on January 12, 1949.[[15]](#cite_note-NOWData_NWS_Las_Vegas,_NV_(VEF)_-_EEDthr-15)[[11]](#cite_note-wrcc-11) The city is also known for moderate to locally severe thunderstorms during the [monsoon](/wiki/North_American_Monsoon "North American Monsoon") season as well as humid conditions.

Needles is served by the [National Weather Service](/wiki/National_Weather_Service "National Weather Service")'s NOAA Weather Radio operating on 162.50 MHz from the Las Vegas National Weather Service.

Major employment in the city is supported by the [BNSF Railway](/wiki/BNSF_Railway "BNSF Railway") (formerly the [Santa Fe Railroad](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Railroad "Santa Fe Railroad")). The depot has been a terminal (crew change point) for the railway since the late 19th century. The railroad company has been the city's main employment source for over a century.

The once smaller nearby communities of [Bullhead City, Arizona](/wiki/Bullhead_City,_Arizona "Bullhead City, Arizona"), [Lake Havasu City, Arizona](/wiki/Lake_Havasu_City,_Arizona "Lake Havasu City, Arizona"), and [Laughlin, Nevada](/wiki/Laughlin,_Nevada "Laughlin, Nevada") have in recent years become larger communities than Needles.

### 2020

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2020")]

The [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") reported that Needles had a population of 4,931. The population density was 161.3 inhabitants per square mile (62.3/km2). The racial makeup of Needles was 67.4% [White](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans"), 2.5% [African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 9.0% [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), 1.2% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), 0.2% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_Americans "Pacific Islander Americans"), 8.4% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 11.3% from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") of any race were 25.5% of the population.[[21]](#cite_note-DP1-22)

The census reported that 99.7% of the population lived in households, 0.3% lived in non-institutionalized group quarters, and no one was institutionalized.[[21]](#cite_note-DP1-22)

There were 2,056 households, out of which 27.2% included children under the age of 18, 36.4% were married-couple households, 9.3% were [cohabiting](/wiki/Cohabitation "Cohabitation") couple households, 31.4% had a female householder with no partner present, and 22.9% had a male householder with no partner present. 30.6% of households were one person, and 13.1% were one person aged 65 or older. The average household size was 2.39.[[21]](#cite_note-DP1-22) There were 1,247 [families](/wiki/Family_(U.S._Census) "Family (U.S. Census)") (60.7% of all households).[[22]](#cite_note-23)

The age distribution was 22.6% under the age of 18, 6.8% aged 18 to 24, 21.4% aged 25 to 44, 28.3% aged 45 to 64, and 20.9% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 44.4 years. For every 100 females, there were 97.7 males.[[21]](#cite_note-DP1-22)

There were 2,743 housing units at an average density of 89.7 units per square mile (34.6 units/km2), of which 2,056 (75.0%) were occupied. Of these, 51.1% were owner-occupied, and 48.9% were occupied by renters.[[21]](#cite_note-DP1-22)

In 2023, the US Census Bureau estimated that the median household income was $39,876, and the [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") was $29,587. About 25.1% of families and 29.1% of the population were below the poverty line.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Needles_California_4_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Needles_California_4_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Needles_California_4_(cropped).jpg)

Broadway Street

The City of Needles was incorporated on October 30, 1913.[[2]](#cite_note-calafco-2) It is a charter city, led by an elected mayor and a city council with six elected members. Mayors serve two-year terms of office, and councilmembers serve four-year terms. The council designates a vice mayor from among its members.[[27]](#cite_note-cc-28) The city council also appoints a city manager who is responsible for the operation of city departments. As of October 2023, the current city manager is Patrick J. Martinez.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

### State and federal representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: State and federal representation")]

In the [California State Legislature](/wiki/California_State_Legislature "California State Legislature"), Needles is in [the 18th senatorial district](/wiki/California%27s_18th_senatorial_district "California's 18th senatorial district"), represented by [Democrat](/wiki/California_Democratic_Party "California Democratic Party") [Steve Padilla](/wiki/Steve_Padilla "Steve Padilla"), and in [the 36th Assembly district](/wiki/California%27s_36th_State_Assembly_district "California's 36th State Assembly district"), represented by [Republican](/wiki/California_Republican_Party "California Republican Party") [Jeff Gonzalez](/wiki/Jeff_Gonzalez "Jeff Gonzalez").[[29]](#cite_note-30)

In the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), Needles is in [California's 25th congressional district](/wiki/California%27s_25th_congressional_district "California's 25th congressional district"), represented by [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") [Raul Ruiz](/wiki/Raul_Ruiz_(politician) "Raul Ruiz (politician)").[[30]](#cite_note-31)

### Proposals for secession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Proposals for secession")]

In 2008, claiming the county had been unwilling to help keep the city's troubled hospital open as a full-service medical facility, the city considered seceding from California and becoming part of neighboring [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"), only a few miles away. The options of attaching itself to the state of [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") or even forming a new county were also considered.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Proposals to change states would require approval from the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") and both state legislatures.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Education")]

The city is in the [Needles Unified School District](/wiki/Needles_Unified_School_District "Needles Unified School District").[[32]](#cite_note-CensusUSSDMap2020-33) Needles' elementary schools and [Needles High School](/wiki/Needles_High_School "Needles High School") are part of the district. The school district is one of the largest in the United States in terms of area with almost 6,000 square miles (16,000 km2) in its boundaries. The district runs from Amboy to Needles, and south to Parker Dam.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit) it had 955 students enrolled.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

The local Needles schools include Katie Hohstadt Elementary School, formerly called 'D' Street School (new home of Needles Head Start, and no longer a regular public school), Vista Colorado Elementary School (grades K–5), Needles Middle School (grades 6–8), Needles High School (grades 9–12), and the Educational Training Center (grades 9–12). Needles High School, due to its distance from other California schools, is a member of the [Nevada Interscholastic Activities Association](/wiki/Nevada_Interscholastic_Activities_Association "Nevada Interscholastic Activities Association"), along with four other similarly placed California schools: [Truckee](/wiki/Truckee_High_School "Truckee High School"), North Tahoe, South Tahoe, and [Coleville](/w/index.php?title=Coleville_High_School&action=edit&redlink=1 "Coleville High School (page does not exist)").

Needles also has two private schools: the Needles Assembly of God Christian School and the Needles Seventh-day Adventist School.

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[Interstate 40](/wiki/Interstate_40 "Interstate 40"), known locally as the Needles Freeway, is the major highway through Needles, connecting [Barstow](/wiki/Barstow,_California "Barstow, California") to the west and [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") to the east. [U.S. Route 95](/wiki/U.S._Route_95 "U.S. Route 95") also enters the city from the east on former Route 66 as a [concurrency](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") with the I-40 freeway, then splits with the Interstate west of the city, and heads north to [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"). The Colorado River Bridge connects Needles directly with [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), via [Mohave County](/wiki/Mohave_County "Mohave County") and [State Route 95](/wiki/Arizona_State_Route_95 "Arizona State Route 95").

[Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak"), the national passenger rail system, provides daily service to [Needles station](/wiki/Needles_(Amtrak_station) "Needles (Amtrak station)"), operating its [Southwest Chief](/wiki/Southwest_Chief "Southwest Chief") between [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") and [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"). It arrives between midnight and 2 am.

Local transit service to the Needles area is provided by [Needles Area Transit](/wiki/Needles_Area_Transit "Needles Area Transit").[[34]](#cite_note-35)

As of August 2, 2016, [Victor Valley Transit Authority](/wiki/Victor_Valley_Transit_Authority "Victor Valley Transit Authority") has service from Needles to Barstow and Victorville on Fridays. To Barstow and Victorville, the bus leaves at 6:15 and arrives at Victorville at 10:30 am. On the reverse trip, buses leave Victorville at 2:30 pm and arrives at Barstow at 7:15 pm.

Vegas Airporter provides service between [Lake Havasu City](/wiki/Lake_Havasu_City,_Arizona "Lake Havasu City, Arizona"), Needles, and [Harry Reid International Airport](/wiki/Harry_Reid_International_Airport "Harry Reid International Airport") in [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas").[[35]](#cite_note-36)

### Public safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Public safety")]

On July 1, 2016, [San Bernardino County Fire Department](/wiki/San_Bernardino_County_Fire_Department "San Bernardino County Fire Department") annexed the City of Needles.[[36]](#cite_note-37) Fire Station 32 provides fire protection to the City of Needles and houses two Type 1 Engine companies, one Type 7 Engine company, one Water Tender and one 28-foot fireboat. The station is staffed full-time with career firefighters.

Since December 1989 the City of Needles has been patrolled by the [San Bernardino County Sheriff's Department](/wiki/San_Bernardino_County_Sheriff%27s_Department "San Bernardino County Sheriff's Department") under the command of Captain Ross Tarangle from the Needles Patrol Station.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

### Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Health")]

Colorado Medical Center was once a full service hospital but at present it is functioning as an [urgent care center](/wiki/Urgent_care_center "Urgent care center").[[38]](#cite_note-39)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Notable people")]

* [Pat Morris](/wiki/Pat_Morris_(politician) "Pat Morris (politician)"), Mayor of [San Bernardino, California](/wiki/San_Bernardino,_California "San Bernardino, California").[[39]](#cite_note-40)
* [Max Rafferty](/wiki/Max_Rafferty "Max Rafferty"), Needles Superintendent of Schools, 1955–1961,[[40]](#cite_note-41) became California [Superintendent of Public Instruction](/wiki/California_Department_of_Education "California Department of Education") 1962–1970.
* [Charles Schulz](/wiki/Charles_Schulz "Charles Schulz"), cartoonist of *[Peanuts](/wiki/Peanuts "Peanuts")*, lived in Needles 1928–30 and made it the residence of [Snoopy](/wiki/Snoopy "Snoopy")'s brother [Spike](/wiki/Snoopy#Siblings "Snoopy").[[7]](#cite_note-VDP_2020-01-19-7)
* [Bess Houdini](/wiki/Bess_Houdini "Bess Houdini"), wife and stage assistant of famed escape artist [Harry Houdini](/wiki/Harry_Houdini "Harry Houdini"), died in Needles in 1943.[[7]](#cite_note-VDP_2020-01-19-7)
* [Yara tav](/wiki/Irataba "Irataba"), leader of the [Mohave people](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people") (1861–1874), born close to the Needles rock formation before the establishment of the town.[[41]](#cite_note-42)
* [Alice Notley](/wiki/Alice_Notley "Alice Notley"), American poet, grew up in Needles.[[42]](#cite_note-43)
* [Natalie Diaz](/wiki/Natalie_Diaz "Natalie Diaz"), American poet and winner of the 2021 Pulitzer Prize for Poetry.[[43]](#cite_note-44)
* [Sam Kinison](/wiki/Sam_Kinison "Sam Kinison"), American stand-up comedian, was killed in a car crash caused by a 17-year-old drunk driver, about 4.3 miles (6.9 kilometers) outside of Needles on April 10, 1992.[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-NYTcrash-46)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

### Books

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Books")]

* In [John Steinbeck](/wiki/John_Steinbeck "John Steinbeck")'s novel *[The Grapes of Wrath](/wiki/The_Grapes_of_Wrath "The Grapes of Wrath")*, the Joad family stops in Needles when they enter California on [Route 66](/wiki/U.S._Route_66 "U.S. Route 66").[[7]](#cite_note-VDP_2020-01-19-7)
* [Leslie Marmon Silko](/wiki/Leslie_Marmon_Silko "Leslie Marmon Silko")'s novel *Gardens in the Dunes* is largely set in and around Needles in the late 19th century.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

### Print

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Print")]

* In the comic strip *[Peanuts](/wiki/Peanuts "Peanuts"),* Snoopy's brother [Spike](/wiki/Snoopy#Siblings "Snoopy") lived in the desert outside Needles.[[7]](#cite_note-VDP_2020-01-19-7) He frequently heads to Needles to partake of the town's nightlife, often running afoul of the local coyotes.

### Recordings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Recordings")]

* In 2004, [John Lowery](/wiki/John_5_(guitarist) "John 5 (guitarist)") (John 5), former guitarist for Marilyn Manson, released his CD *[Vertigo](/wiki/Vertigo_(John_5_album) "Vertigo (John 5 album)")*, in which the first track is entitled "Needles CA".
* The town is mentioned in the lyrics of [Hoyt Axton](/wiki/Hoyt_Axton "Hoyt Axton")'s "[Never Been to Spain](/wiki/Never_Been_to_Spain "Never Been to Spain")"; the song was a hit for [Three Dog Night](/wiki/Three_Dog_Night "Three Dog Night") in 1972 and was also performed by Elvis and Waylon Jennings:

> Well I never been to England, but I kinda like the Beatles. Well, I headed for Las Vegas, only made it out to Needles. Can you feel it? Must be real. It feels so good!

* [Izzy Stradlin](/wiki/Izzy_Stradlin "Izzy Stradlin")'s 1999 album *[Ride On](/wiki/Ride_On_(Izzy_Stradlin_album) "Ride On (Izzy Stradlin album)")* includes a track entitled "Needles" about his love of visiting the town.

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Television")]

* In October 2006, two students and two teachers from Needles High School were invited to Washington, D.C. to meet with the Under Secretary of Defense, in which they spoke of the new program at Needles High School called MOCK National Security Workshop. The students were also interviewed for the nationwide, fifteen-minute television news show, *[Channel One News](/wiki/Channel_One_News "Channel One News")*; the episode was aired on October 25.
* Needles was the main site of a 2009 *[UFO Hunters](/wiki/UFO_Hunters "UFO Hunters")* episode investigating a supposed [UFO](/wiki/UFO "UFO") Crash.
* [Needles High School](/wiki/Needles_High_School "Needles High School") was on a *[School Pride](/wiki/School_Pride "School Pride")* television episode on November 12, 2010.

### Other connections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Other connections")]

* In late 2000 to early 2001, skateboarder [Tony Hawk](/wiki/Tony_Hawk "Tony Hawk") donated $20,000 to the Needles Skate Park, which is still in use today[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Needles,_California&action=edit). Hawk was present for the grand opening of the park in January 2004.[[47]](#cite_note-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49)
* Needles (and the surrounding area) was the scene for the hit 1988 [post-apocalyptic](/wiki/Post-apocalyptic "Post-apocalyptic") computer [RPG](/wiki/Role-playing_game "Role-playing game") *[Wasteland](/wiki/Wasteland_(computer_game) "Wasteland (computer game)")*.
* Murals were painted of [U.S. Route 66](/wiki/U.S._Route_66 "U.S. Route 66"), which passed through Needles on its way between [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") and [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles").[[49]](#cite_note-50)
